[Esbonio](https://docs.esbon.io/)<br/><br/># Esbonio Preview Issues with Sphinx and Python Environment<br/><br/>## Common Problem<br/>Users have reported esbonio preview not working despite Sphinx successfully building projects. One documented case involved a MacBook Pro M1 with macOS Ventura where the preview feature would timeout and show server connection errors, while the same setup worked fine on Windows.[1]<br/><br/>## Key Configuration Areas to Check<br/><br/>**Sphinx Build Environment**: Make sure your Sphinx build environment is properly configured. Esbonio allows you to configure the Sphinx build environment through settings like `buildDir`, `confDir`, `srcDir`, and other build parameters.[3]<br/><br/>**Python Path**: If you're using a custom Python environment (like a conda environment), ensure the Python path is correctly configured. The `esbonio.sphinx.pythonPath` option allows you to specify paths to inject into the target environment.[3]<br/><br/>## Preview Implementation Details<br/><br/>Live previews only work with builders that produce HTML output.[2] To enable previews, your editor needs to invoke the `esbonio.server.previewFile` command, which generates and opens the preview for a given file. The server looks up the corresponding output HTML file and attempts to open it.[2]<br/><br/>## Troubleshooting Steps<br/><br/>Check your logging configuration to get more detailed error messages. Enable debug-level logging with `esbonio.logging.level` set to `debug` to diagnose what's happening during the preview process.[3] This is particularly important on macOS where preview logging may not work as expected with some extension versions. |

{
  "url": "https://docs.esbon.io/en/latest/integrating/previews.html",
  "markdown": "Live Previews - Esbonio\n\n# Live Previews¶\n\nNote\n\nCurrently live previews are only implemented for builders that produce HTML output.\n\n&#96;esbonio&#96; is able to offer a preview of your documentation that is automatically updated as you work.\n\nWhile the preview functionality tries to leverage existing LSP methods where possible, there are some features that require extra methods to enable. This page provides all the details you will need to enable live previews in your language client of choice.\n\n## Basic Previews¶\n\nTo trigger a preview for a file, your language client will need to invoke the following command using the [workspace/executeCommand](https://microsoft.github.io/language-server-protocol/specifications/lsp/3.17/specification/#workspace_executeCommand) request from the Language Server Protocol.\n\nesboino.server.previewFile¶\n\nGenerate and open the preview for the given file.\n\nThis command requires the following arguments\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n[\n  {\"uri\": \"file:///path/to/file/to/preview.rst\"}\n]\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nThe server will then look up the output file that corresponds with the given uri, if no such file exists, the server will do nothing and return a&#96;null&#96; response.\n\nAssuming the output file exists and your language client supports it the server will send a [window/showDocument](https://microsoft.github.io/language-server-protocol/specifications/lsp/3.17/specification/#window_showDocument) request to open the resulting HTML file in your web browser.\n\nHowever, to handle the case where your client does not support [window/showDocument](https://microsoft.github.io/language-server-protocol/specifications/lsp/3.17/specification/#window_showDocument) the server will also return the following object allowing you to open the corresponding uri yourself.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n{\"uri\": \"http://localhost:1234/preview.html?ws=56789\"}\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nImplementing this single command will give you a fairly good preview experience.\n\nThe preview will automatically reload each time a Sphinx build completes.\n\nAssuming your client supports the&#96;selection&#96; field of a&#96;window/showDocument&#96; request, scrolling the preview window will automatically scroll your editor window to match.\n\nTo preview another file, send another&#96;esbonio.server.previewFile&#96; command. Depending on your language client, you might be able to [automate this](https://github.com/swyddfa/esbonio/blob/12c9bd93b9b43eaa5538a0dc8047e966dcbf68e8/code/src/node/preview.ts#L38)\n\n## Synchronised Scrolling¶\n\nHowever, to implement full synchronised scrolling you will need your client to send the following custom notification each time you scroll your editor.\n\nview/scroll¶\n\nScroll the preview to reveal the given line number. This requires the following parameters\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n{\"uri\": \"file:///path/to/file/to/preview.rst\", \"line\": 10}\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nWhere&#96;line&#96; is the line number visible at the very top of your editor window. The&#96;uri&#96; of the current file is also required since output files can contain the contents of one or more input files (e.g. using the&#96;.. include::&#96; directive).\n\n## Example Implementation¶\n\nTip\n\nFor a more complete example, see the preview implementation in the [Esbonio VSCode Extension](https://github.com/swyddfa/esbonio/blob/develop/code/src/node/preview.ts)\n\nThe following is just enough configuration to enable live previews with synchronised scrolling in Neovim. There are likely some quality of life improvements you would want to make to this, but it’s enough to get something up and running.\n\nThis example\n\nAdds a&#96;:EsbonioPreviewFile&#96; command to generate the preview for the current file\n\nRelies on Neovim’s built in [window/showDocument](https://microsoft.github.io/language-server-protocol/specifications/lsp/3.17/specification/#window_showDocument) handler both to open the preview in your default web browser and synchronise the preview’s scroll state with the editor.\n\nSets up an autocmd for the&#96;WinScrolled&#96; event to synchronise the editor’s scroll state with the preview.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\nrequire('lspconfig').esbonio.setup {\n  commands = {\n    EsbonioPreviewFile = {\n      preview_file,\n      description = 'Preview Current File',\n    },\n  },\n}\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nWhere the&#96;preview_file&#96; function sends the&#96;esbonio.server.previewFile&#96; command and sets up the&#96;WinScrolled&#96; autocommand.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\nfunction preview_file()\n  local params = {\n    command = 'esbonio.server.previewFile',\n    arguments = {\n      { uri = vim.uri_from_bufnr(0) },\n    },\n  }\n\n  local clients = require('lspconfig.util').get_lsp_clients {\n    bufnr = vim.api.nvim_get_current_buf(),\n    name = 'esbonio',\n  }\n  for _, client in ipairs(clients) do\n    client.request('workspace/executeCommand', params, nil, 0)\n  end\n\n  local augroup = vim.api.nvim_create_augroup(\"EsbonioSyncScroll\", { clear = true })\n  vim.api.nvim_create_autocmd({\"WinScrolled\"}, {\n    callback = scroll_view, group = augroup, buffer = 0\n  })\nend\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nAnd the&#96;scroll_view&#96; command sends the required&#96;view/scroll&#96; notification with the server.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\nfunction scroll_view(event)\n  local esbonio = vim.lsp.get_active_clients({ bufnr = 0, name = 'esbonio' })[1]\n  local view = vim.fn.winsaveview()\n\n  local params = { uri = vim.uri_from_bufnr(0),  line = view.topline }\n  esbonio.notify('view/scroll', params)\nend\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;"
}

{
  "url": "https://docs.esbon.io/en/latest/usage/getting-started.html",
  "markdown": "Getting Started - Esbonio\n\n# Getting Started¶\n\nThis tutorial will guide you through the process of using the Esbonio language server with VSCode for the first time. Even if VSCode is not your editor of choice, it is still recommended that you read through this guide as the concepts covered here will apply to other editors.\n\nTo follow along you will need either\n\nVSCode, Git and Python installed on your machine, or\n\nA GitHub account with access to [Codespaces](https://github.com/features/codespaces)\n\n## Initial Setup¶\n\nThis tutorial uses a [demo project](https://github.com/swyddfa/esbonio-demo) to illustrate the features provided by Esbonio, so before we can begin we need to get this project setup on your machine.\n\nThe setup steps will be different depending on how you intend to follow along\n\nVSCode: Follow these steps if you are using a VSCode\n\nVSCode + Dev Container: Follow these steps if you are using VSCode, but want to use a [devcontainer](https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/devcontainers/containers) for the project’s environment.\n\nCodespaces: Follow these steps if you don’t have VSCode or would prefer to use your browser.\n\nVSCode\n\nOpen a terminal and clone the [demo repository](https://github.com/swyddfa/esbonio-demo):\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n$ git clone https://github.com/swyddfa/esbonio-demo\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nOpen the demo repository in VSCode:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n$ cd esbonio-demo &amp;&amp; code .\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nOpen the Extensions sidebar (Ctrl+Shift+X) and search for&#96;esbonio&#96;. You should see the “Esbonio” extension at the top of the list, with an icon matching the logo you see on this documentation site.\n\nInstead of clicking the Install button, click the down arrow to the right of the Install text and pick the Install Pre-Release button.\n\nVSCode + Dev Container\n\nOpen a terminal and clone the [demo repository](https://github.com/swyddfa/esbonio-demo):\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n$ git clone https://github.com/swyddfa/esbonio-demo\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nOpen the demo repository in VSCode:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n$ cd esbonio-demo &amp;&amp; code .\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nAssuming that you already have the [Dev Containers](https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=ms-vscode-remote.remote-containers) extension installed, you should be prompted to reopen the repository in a container. Alternatively, you can run the Dev Containers: Reopen in Container command through the command palette (Ctrl+Shift+P)\n\nVSCode will automatically install the Esbonio extension as part of the container setup process.\n\nCodespaces\n\nOpen the repository for the [demo project](https://github.com/swyddfa/esbonio-demo) in your browser\n\nSign in to GitHub if you have not done so already\n\nClick the green &lt;&gt; Code button\n\nSelect the Codespaces tab in the popup dialog and click the green Create codespace on main button.\n\n## Open a Preview¶\n\nBy this point you should have the demo project open and the Esbonio extension installed.\n\nOpen the Explorer sidebar (Ctrl+Shift+E) and click the&#96;index.rst&#96; file.\n\nOpen a preview of this file by either\n\nClicking the icon in header that looks like a book\n\nHitting the Ctrl+K V keyboard shortcut\n\nRunning the Esbonio: Preview Documentation in Split Window command through the command palette (Ctrl+Shift+P)\n\nAfter a few seconds the Esbonio extension will have built the documentation in the background and be showing the result in the new side window.\n\nIf you are using Codespaces…\n\nPreviews in Esbonio work by starting Python’s built-in&#96;http.server&#96; in your project’s build directory and are hosted on&#96;localhost: &#96;- something that shouldn’t normally work in a Codespace. Thankfully, GitHub will automatically expose local ports via your Codespace’s URL e.g.&#96;https://codespace-name-q99gp466q62xwqw.github.dev: /&#96;\n\nUnfortunately, the browser will initially block Esbonio’s preview pane from accessing this URL with a message like&#96;This content is blocked. Contact the site owner to fix the issue&#96;.\n\nTo fix this, we first need to open these Codespace URLs in a new tab\n\nIf VSCode’s bottom panel is not already open, open it with the Ctrl+J shortcut.\n\nSelect the Ports tab, you should see two URLs listed in a table.\n\nClick on the globe icon next to each of the links to open them in a new tab\n\nOne of the links will take you to the documentation preview\n\nThe other will be an error message like&#96;Failed to open a WebSocket connection&#96;. This is expected, you can ignore this\n\nClose both of the tabs and return to the Codespace\n\nClose the Esbonio Preview window and re-open it using one of the methods mentioned above.\n\nGo back to the&#96;index.rst&#96; file and replace the&#96;.. Write a message here&#96; comment with&#96;Hello, World!&#96;.\n\nNotice how after a few seconds the preview will update showing the result of the changes you made.\n\n## Fixing the Project Configuration¶\n\nBy this point you may have noticed that there are some issues with this project. Open VSCode’s Problems tab (Ctrl+Shift+M) and you will see a number of errors including\n\nCould not import extension sphinx_design (exception: No module named ‘sphinx_design’)\n\nNo theme named ‘furo’ found (missing theme.toml?)\n\nUnknown directive type “grid”\n\nAlso if you compare your current preview to the [published version](https://demo.esbon.io/en/latest/) of the demo documentation site, you can clearly see that Esbonio is not using the right theme!\n\nThis is because Esbonio is currently using a fallback environment which does not have access to all of the dependencies necessary to build the demo project. While Esbonio is able to work around issues like this, it will work best when we are able to provide it access to a complete environment.\n\nLet’s create a virtual environment containing the required dependencies.\n\nOpen the Terminal (Ctrl+&#96;) and create a new virtual environment:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n$ python -m venv env\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nActivate the environment and install the demo project’s dependencies:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n$ source env/bin/activate\n(env) $ python -m pip install -r requirements.txt\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nWith the environment created, we now need to tell Esbonio to use it\n\nOpen the demo project’s&#96;pyproject.toml&#96; file and add the following&#96;pythonCommand&#96; to the configuration\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n  [tool.esbonio.sphinx]\n  buildCommand = [\"sphinx-build\", \"-M\", \"dirhtml\", \".\", \"./_build\"]\n+ pythonCommand = [\"${venv:env}\"]\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nSave the file, Esbonio will detect the configuration change and automatically restart its background Sphinx process within the newly created environment\n\nRemove the&#96;Hello, World!&#96; text you added in the previous section. The next time the preview refreshes you will see that Esbonio is now using the correct theme for this project and that the errors mentioned at the start of this section have been fixed!\n\n## Next Steps¶\n\nThat’s all you need to get started, you should now be able to make use of Esbonio in your own projects!\n\nIf you like, you can continue exploring the demo project to discover some of the other features Esbonio provides by clicking either\n\nthe&#96;reStructuredText Demo&#96; card, or\n\nthe&#96;MyST Demo&#96; card\n\nin the preview pane. Esbonio will open the corresponding section where you will be taken on a tour through the supported features of your selected syntax.\n\nSee also\n\n[How To Configure the Sphinx Build Environment](https://docs.esbon.io/en/latest/usage/howto/configure-the-sphinx-build-env.html#lsp-configure-sphinx-build-env)\n\nEsbonio is able to work with more than just virtual environments, this guide will show you how to integrate it with other environment managers like poetry.\n\n[How To Configure the Sphinx Build](https://docs.esbon.io/en/latest/usage/howto/configure-the-sphinx-build-cmd.html#lsp-configure-sphinx-build-cmd)\n\nThis guide will provide more examples of the&#96;esbonio.sphinx.buildCommand&#96; option\n\n[Editor Integration](https://docs.esbon.io/en/latest/integrating/getting-started.html#editor-integration)\n\nThis section provides details on how to integrate Esbonio with other text editors"
}

{
  "url": "https://docs.esbon.io/en/latest/usage/howto/configure-the-sphinx-build-env.html",
  "markdown": "How To Configure the Sphinx Build Environment - Esbonio\n\n# How To Configure the Sphinx Build Environment¶\n\nEsbonio works by creating a background Sphinx process which it can use to build your documentation and extract information from it. This of course, requires&#96;esbonio&#96; being able to execute this process within the correct Python environment.\n\nSince there are many ways to define and manage Python environments,&#96;esbonio&#96; needs you to tell it how to run the&#96;python&#96; command so that it has access to the correct dependencies. This is typically done by setting the [esbonio.sphinx.pythonCommand](https://docs.esbon.io/en/latest/usage/reference/configuration.html#esbonio.sphinx.pythonCommand) option in your project’s&#96;pyproject.toml&#96; file.\n\nIf the command is not&#96;python&#96;, then it must accept additional parameters (e.g.&#96;-m sphinx&#96;) and pass these to the Python interpreter.\n\n&#96;esbonio&#96; sets the environment variable&#96;PYTHONPATH&#96; for the python interpreter, therefore, the command must not replace or clear&#96;PYTHONPATH&#96;. It may, however, extend the environment variable with additional entries.\n\n## Basic Usage¶\n\nMost of the time providing just the command to invoke as a list of strings is all that is required. Below are some examples on how you would set the [esbonio.sphinx.pythonCommand](https://docs.esbon.io/en/latest/usage/reference/configuration.html#esbonio.sphinx.pythonCommand) option depending on your choice of environment manager tool.\n\n### Hatch¶\n\nIf you use [hatch](https://hatch.pypa.io/latest/) to define your environments\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n[tool.hatch.envs.docs]\ndependencies = [\n   \"sphinx\",\n   \"sphinx-design\",\n   \"furo\",\n   \"myst-parser\",\n]\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nThen you should set [esbonio.sphinx.pythonCommand](https://docs.esbon.io/en/latest/usage/reference/configuration.html#esbonio.sphinx.pythonCommand) to\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n[tool.esbonio.sphinx]\npythonCommand = [\"hatch\", \"-e\", \"docs\", \"run\", \"python\"]\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n### Pipenv¶\n\nIf your project uses [Pipenv](https://pipenv.pypa.io/en/latest/index.html) for its dependency management\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n[[source]]\nurl = \"https://pypi.org/simple\"\nverify_ssl = true\nname = \"pypi\"\n\n[packages]\nfuro = \"*\"\nsphinx = \"*\"\nsphinx-design = \"*\"\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nThen [esbonio.sphinx.pythonCommand](https://docs.esbon.io/en/latest/usage/reference/configuration.html#esbonio.sphinx.pythonCommand) should be set to\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n[tool.esbonio.sphinx]\npythonCommand = [\"pipenv\", \"run\", \"python\"]\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n### Poetry¶\n\nGiven a set of dependencies managed through [Poetry](https://python-poetry.org/)\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n[tool.poetry.dependencies]\npython = \"&gt;=3.9\"\ncattrs = \"&gt;=23.1.2\"\nlsprotocol = \"2024.0.0a2\"\nwebsockets = { version = \"&gt;=11.0.3\", optional = true }\n\n[tool.poetry.group.docs.dependencies]\nmyst-parser = \"&gt;=2.0\"\nsphinx = \"&gt;=7.1.2\"\nsphinx-design = \"&gt;=0.5.0\"\nsphinx-rtd-theme = \"&gt;=1.3.0\"\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nYou will first need to make sure that&#96;poetry&#96; has created the environment with the required dependencies\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n$ poetry install --with docs\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nThen you should set [esbonio.sphinx.pythonCommand](https://docs.esbon.io/en/latest/usage/reference/configuration.html#esbonio.sphinx.pythonCommand) to\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n[tool.esbonio.sphinx]\npythonCommand = [\"poetry\", \"run\", \"python\"]\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n### uv¶\n\nIf you use [uv](https://docs.astral.sh/uv/) and a&#96;pyproject.toml&#96; file to manage your dependencies\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n[project]\ndependencies = [\n    \"attrs&gt;=24.3.0\",\n    \"cattrs&gt;=23.1.2\",\n    \"lsprotocol==2025.0.0\",\n]\n\n[dependency-groups]\ndocs = [\n    \"furo&gt;=2024.8.6\",\n    \"myst-parser&gt;=2.0\",\n    \"sphinx&gt;=7.1.2\",\n    \"sphinx-design&gt;=0.5.0\",\n]\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nThen you should set [esbonio.sphinx.pythonCommand](https://docs.esbon.io/en/latest/usage/reference/configuration.html#esbonio.sphinx.pythonCommand) to\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n[tool.esbonio.sphinx]\npythonCommand = [\"uv\", \"run\", \"--group\", \"docs\", \"python\"]\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nAlternatively, you can specify the dependencies direct with a&#96;uv run&#96; command\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n[tool.esbonio.sphinx]\npythonCommand = [\n    \"uv\", \"run\", \"--no-project\",\n    \"--with\", \"sphinx\",\n    \"--with\", \"myst-parser\",\n    \"python\"\n]\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nDon’t forget the&#96;--no-project&#96; flag, otherwise&#96;uv&#96; will attempt to include dependencies from the&#96;pyproject.toml&#96; file also!\n\n### venv / virtualenv¶\n\nAssuming you already have an envrionment that you use to build your documentation\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n(venv) $ python -m pip list\nPackage                       Version    Editable project location\n----------------------------- ---------- -------------------------------------------------\n...\nSphinx                        7.1.2\nsphinx_design                 0.5.0\nsphinx-rtd-theme              2.0.0\nsphinxcontrib-applehelp       1.0.4\nsphinxcontrib-devhelp         1.0.2\nsphinxcontrib-htmlhelp        2.0.1\nsphinxcontrib-jquery          4.1\nsphinxcontrib-jsmath          1.0.1\nsphinxcontrib-qthelp          1.0.3\nsphinxcontrib-serializinghtml 1.1.5\nurllib3                       2.1.0\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nThen set [esbonio.sphinx.pythonCommand](https://docs.esbon.io/en/latest/usage/reference/configuration.html#esbonio.sphinx.pythonCommand) to the full path to the&#96;python&#96; executable contained in the environment (which will be slightly different depending on your operating system)\n\nLinux / macOS\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n[tool.esbonio.sphinx]\npythonCommand = [\"/home/user/Projects/myproject/venv/bin/python\"]\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nWindows\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n[tool.esbonio.sphinx]\npythonCommand = [\"C:\\\\Users\\\\user\\\\Projects\\\\myproject\\\\venv\\\\Scripts\\\\python.exe\"]\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nAlternatively, you can use the&#96;${venv:}&#96; configuration variable, this allows you to provide just the path to the&#96;venv&#96; folder and&#96;esbonio&#96; will expand it to the correct path to the Python executable for your platform.&#96; &#96; can either be an absolute path, or relative to the folder containing your&#96;pyproject.toml&#96; file.\n\nFor example, say your project had the following structure\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n$ tree myproject\nmyproject\n├── docs\n│   └── pyproject.toml\n└── venv\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nThen your&#96;pyproject.toml&#96; might look something like the following.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n[tool.esbonio.sphinx]\npythonCommand = [\"${venv:../venv}\"]\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n## Advanced Usage¶\n\nThere are situations where you might need more control over how the background Sphinx process is launched. In which case [esbonio.sphinx.pythonCommand](https://docs.esbon.io/en/latest/usage/reference/configuration.html#esbonio.sphinx.pythonCommand) accepts an object allowing you to provide additional information.\n\nWarning\n\nIf you use the VSCode extension, when using this expanded configuration format, you must provide a value for the&#96;PYTHONPATH&#96; environment variable (even if it is empty). Otherwise the extension’s fallback environment will take priority over your intended environment and will lead to unexpected results.\n\nExample config:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n[tool.esbonio.sphinx.pythonCommand]\ncommand = [\"uv\", \"run\", \"python\"]\nenv = { PYTHONPATH = \"\", MY_ENV_VAR = \"value\" }\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nSee [this issue](https://github.com/swyddfa/esbonio/issues/1074) for more details.\n\n### Environment Variables¶\n\nIf you need to set additional environment variables you can provide an&#96;env&#96; dictionary alongside the&#96;command&#96;\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n[tool.esbonio.sphinx.pythonCommand]\ncommand = [\"uv\", \"run\", \"python\"]\nenv = { MY_ENV_VAR = \"value\" }\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n### Working Directory¶\n\nBy default,&#96;esbonio&#96; will launch the background process from the directory containing your&#96;pyproject.toml&#96; file. To change this you can provide the&#96;cwd&#96; option alongside your&#96;command&#96;\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n[tool.esbonio.sphinx.pythonCommand]\ncommand = [\"uv\", \"run\", \"python\"]\ncwd = \"/path/to/docs\"\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nTo specify a path relative to the location of your&#96;pyproject.toml&#96; file, use the&#96;${scopeFsPath}&#96; variable\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n[tool.esbonio.sphinx.pythonCommand]\ncommand = [\"uv\", \"run\", \"python\"]\ncwd = \"${scopeFsPath}/docs\"\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;"
}
```

{
  "plan": "# Esbonio Preview Sorun Giderme Planı\n\n## Mevcut Durum\n\n- **Proje yapısı**: [Makefile](Makefile) ile &#96;source/&#96; (conf.py burada) ve &#96;build/&#96; dizinleri kullanılıyor\n- **Python ortamı**: &#96;belgeler-truba&#96; conda env (&#96;/Users/iguzel/miniforge3/envs/belgeler-truba/bin/python&#96;)\n- **Esbonio yapılandırması**: Projede &#96;pyproject.toml&#96; veya &#96;.vscode/settings.json&#96; yok – Esbonio tamamen varsayılanlarla çalışıyor\n\n## Olası Nedenler\n\n### 1. Python ortamı (en olası)\n\nEsbonio, Sphinx’i arka planda çalıştırır ve bunun için doğru Python ortamını kullanması gerekir. Projede &#96;pythonCommand&#96; tanımlanmadığı için Esbonio:\n\n- Python extension’ın seçili interpreter’ını veya\n- Fallback ortamı (çoğunlukla &#96;python&#96; ile bulunan ilk ortam)\n\nkullanır. Terminal’de &#96;belgeler-truba&#96; aktif olsa bile, Cursor/VS Code’ta **workspace Python interpreter** olarak bu ortam seçilmemiş olabilir.\n\n[source/conf.py](source/conf.py) içindeki extension’lar (&#96;sphinx_design&#96;, &#96;sphinx_copybutton&#96;, &#96;sphinx_tabs&#96;, &#96;sphinx_togglebutton&#96;, &#96;sphinx_last_updated_by_git&#96;) fallback ortamda olmayabilir; bu da Sphinx build’in hata vermesine ve preview’ın açılmamasına yol açar.\n\n### 2. Sphinx dizin yapılandırması\n\nProje yapısı:\n\n- &#96;confDir&#96; = &#96;source/&#96;\n- &#96;srcDir&#96; = &#96;source/&#96;\n- &#96;buildDir&#96; = &#96;build/html&#96; (Sphinx &#96;-M html source build&#96; ile çıktıyı buraya yazar)\n\nEsbonio bu dizinleri kendisi keşfetmeye çalışır; &#96;conf.py&#96; klasik yerde (&#96;source/&#96;) olduğu için genelde doğru bulur. Ancak &#96;buildDir&#96;’in HTML çıktı dizinine (&#96;build/html&#96;) işaret etmesi gerekir.\n\n### 3. Mac’te eski Esbonio sürümü (daha az olası)\n\n[GitHub Issue #790](https://github.com/swyddfa/esbonio/issues/790): Mac’te preview server multiprocessing nedeniyle timeout verebiliyordu. Esbonio 0.16.5 ile düzeltildi. Eski bir sürüm kullanılıyorsa bu etki olabilir.\n\n---\n\n## Önerilen Çözüm Adımları\n\n### Adım 1: Python interpreter kontrolü (öncelikli)\n\n1. Cursor’da **Command Palette** (Cmd+Shift+P) → **Python: Select Interpreter**\n2. &#96;belgeler-truba&#96; ortamını seçin: &#96;/Users/iguzel/miniforge3/envs/belgeler-truba/bin/python&#96;\n3. Gerekirse Cursor’u yeniden başlatın\n\nBu, Esbonio’nun Sphinx’i hangi Python ile çalıştırdığını belirleyecek.\n\n### Adım 2: Gerekli paketlerin ortamda olduğunu doğrulama\n\nTerminal’de (belgeler-truba aktifken):\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\npython -c \"import sphinx; import sphinx_design; import sphinx_tabs; print('OK')\"\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nHata alırsanız eksik paketleri yükleyin:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\npip install sphinx sphinx_design sphinx_copybutton sphinx_tabs sphinx_togglebutton sphinx_last_updated_by_git\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n### Adım 3: Manuel Sphinx build denemesi\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\ncd /Users/iguzel/Desktop/Belgeler\nmake html\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nBuild başarılı olursa &#96;build/html/&#96; içinde HTML dosyaları oluşur. - `buildCommand`: aynı ortamdaki `sphinx-build` ile HTML derleme

[Changelog #](https://docs.esbon.io/en/esbonio-language-server-v0.16.4/changelog.html)<br/><br/># Esbonio Configuration: confDir, srcDir, buildDir, outdir, and Preview Settings<br/><br/>## Core Sphinx Configuration Options<br/><br/>Esbonio uses several key directory configuration parameters for Sphinx projects:[1]<br/><br/>- **confDir**: The directory containing your Sphinx configuration file (conf.py)<br/>- **srcDir**: The source directory where your reStructuredText files are located<br/>- **buildDir**: The output directory where Sphinx builds your documentation<br/><br/>These are project-level configuration options that can be set via workspace/configuration requests, pyproject.toml files, or initializationOptions.[1]<br/><br/>## Preview Configuration<br/><br/>Esbonio offers live preview functionality for HTML output.[2] To enable previews:<br/><br/>- Use the `esbonio.server.previewFile` command to generate and open previews for files[2]<br/>- The preview automatically reloads each time a Sphinx build completes[2]<br/>- Previews work by starting Python's built-in `http.server` in your project's build directory, typically hosted on `localhost`[3]<br/><br/>Additional preview-related configuration includes `esbonio.preview.showLineMarkers`, which when enabled reveals the source URI and line number for HTML elements under the cursor.[1]<br/><br/>## Configuration Scopes and Sources<br/><br/>Esbonio supports both **global** (server-wide) and **project** (single project) configuration scopes. Configuration can be set from three sources in priority order: workspace/configuration requests, pyproject.toml files, and initializationOptions, with higher-priority sources overriding lower ones.[1] |

{
  "url": "https://docs.esbon.io/en/latest/usage/reference/configuration.html",
  "markdown": "Configuration - Esbonio\n\n# Configuration¶\n\nEsbonio provides a flexible configuration system, allowing you to adapt the server to fit your project’s needs.\n\n## Scopes¶\n\nConfiguration values are assigned one of the following scopes\n\n&#96;global&#96;: For options that apply to the entire language server e.g. logging level.\n\n&#96;project&#96;: For options that apply to a single project e.g. a&#96;sphinx-build&#96; command.\n\n## Sources¶\n\nThe language server supports reading configuration values from the following sources.\n\n(Priortiy) Source\n\nSupported Config Scopes\n\nNotes\n\n(1) [workspace/configuration](https://microsoft.github.io/language-server-protocol/specifications/lsp/3.17/specification/#workspace_configuration)\n\n&#96;global&#96;,&#96;project&#96;\n\n(2)&#96;pyproject.toml&#96; files\n\n&#96;project&#96;\n\n(3)&#96;initialzationOptions&#96;\n\n&#96;global&#96;,&#96;project&#96;\n\nSettings for multiple projects are not supported.\n\nWhen determining the value to assign to a particular configuration option, Esbonio will merge options given by the sources in order of descending priority i.e. options set via [workspace/configuration](https://microsoft.github.io/language-server-protocol/specifications/lsp/3.17/specification/#workspace_configuration) requests will override all other sources.\n\n## Options¶\n\nBelow are all the configuration options supported by the server and their effects.\n\nCompletion\n\nDeveloper\n\nLogging\n\nSphinx\n\nPreview\n\n### Completion¶\n\nThe following options affect completion suggestions.\n\nglobal string esbonio.server.completion.preferredInsertBehavior¶\n\nControls how completions behave when accepted, the following values are supported.\n\n&#96;replace&#96;(default)\n\nAccepted completions will replace existing text, allowing the server to rewrite the current line in place. This allows the server to return all possible completions within the current context. In this mode the server will set the&#96;textEdit&#96; field of a&#96;CompletionItem&#96;.\n\n&#96;insert&#96;\n\nAccepted completions will append to existing text rather than replacing it. Since rewriting is not possible, only the completions that are compatible with any existing text will be returned. In this mode the server will set the&#96;insertText&#96; field of a&#96;CompletionItem&#96; which should work better with editors that do no support&#96;textEdits&#96;.\n\n### Developer¶\n\nThe following options are useful when extending or working on the language server\n\nglobal boolean esbonio.server.showDeprecationWarnings¶\n\nDeveloper flag which, when enabled, the server will publish any deprecation warnings as diagnostics.\n\nglobal boolean esbonio.server.enableDevTools¶\n\nEnable [lsp-devtools](https://swyddfa.github.io/lsp-devtools/docs/latest/en/) integration for the language server itself.\n\nglobal boolean esbonio.sphinx.enableDevTools¶\n\nEnable [lsp-devtools](https://swyddfa.github.io/lsp-devtools/docs/latest/en/) integration for the Sphinx subprocess started by the language server.\n\nglobal string[] esbonio.sphinx.pythonPath¶\n\nList of paths to use when constructing the value of&#96;PYTHONPATH&#96;. Used to inject the sphinx agent into the target environment.”\n\nglobal boolean esbonio.preview.showLineMarkers¶\n\nWhen enabled, reveal the source uri and line number (if possible) for the html element under the cursor.\n\n### Logging¶\n\nThe following options control the logging output of the language server.\n\nglobal string esbonio.logging.level¶\n\nSets the default level of log messages emitted by the server. The following values are accepted, sorted in the order from least to most verbose.\n\n&#96;critical&#96;\n\n&#96;fatal&#96;\n\n&#96;error&#96;(default)\n\n&#96;warning&#96;\n\n&#96;info&#96;\n\n&#96;debug&#96;\n\nglobal string esbonio.logging.format¶\n\nSets the default format string to apply to log messages. This can be any valid [%-style](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#old-string-formatting) format string, referencing valid [LogRecord attributes](https://docs.python.org/3/library/logging.html#logrecord-attributes)\n\nDefault value:&#96;[%(name)s]: %(message)s&#96;\n\nglobal string esbonio.logging.filepath¶\n\nIf set, record log messages in the given filepath (relative to the server’s working directory)\n\nglobal boolean esbonio.logging.stderr¶\n\nIf&#96;True&#96;(the default), the server will print log messages to the process’ stderr\n\nglobal boolean esbonio.logging.window¶\n\nIf&#96;True&#96;, the server will send messages to the client as [window/logMessage](https://microsoft.github.io/language-server-protocol/specifications/lsp/3.17/specification/#window_logMessage) notifications\n\nglobal object esbonio.logging.config¶\n\nThis is an object used to override the default logging configuration for specific, named loggers. Keys in the object are the names of loggers to override, values are a dictionary that can contain the following fields\n\n&#96;level&#96; if present, overrides the value of&#96;esbonio.logging.level&#96;\n\n&#96;format&#96; if present, overrides the value of&#96;esbonio.logging.format&#96;\n\n&#96;filepath&#96; if present, overrides the value of&#96;esbonio.logging.filepath&#96;\n\n&#96;stderr&#96; if present, overrides the value of&#96;esbonio.logging.stderr&#96;\n\n&#96;window&#96; if present, overrides the value of&#96;esbonio.logging.window&#96;\n\n#### Examples¶\n\nThe following is equivalent to the server’s default logging configuration:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n{\n  \"esbonio\": {\n    \"logging\": {\n      \"level\": \"error\",\n      \"format\": \"[%(name)s]: %(message)s\",\n      \"stderr\": true,\n      \"config\": {\n        \"sphinx\": {\n          \"level\": \"info\",\n          \"format\": \"%(message)s\"\n        }\n      }\n    }\n  }\n}\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nThis sets the default log level to&#96;debug&#96; and dials back or redirects the output from some of the noisier loggers:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n{\n  \"esbonio\": {\n    \"logging\": {\n      \"level\": \"debug\",\n      \"config\": {\n        \"esbonio.Configuration\": {\n          \"level\": \"info\"\n        },\n        \"esbonio.PreviewServer\": {\n          \"filepath\": \"http.log\",\n          \"stderr\": false\n        },\n        \"esbonio.WebviewServer\": {\n          \"level\": \"error\"\n        }\n      }\n    }\n  }\n}\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n#### Loggers¶\n\nThe following table summarises (some of) the available loggers and the type of messages they report\n\nName\n\nDescription\n\n&#96;esbonio&#96;\n\nMessages coming from&#96;esbonio&#96; itself that do not belong anywhere else\n\n&#96;esbonio.Configuration&#96;\n\nMessages about merging configuration from multiple sources and notifying the rest of the server when values change.\n\n&#96;esbonio.PreviewManager&#96;\n\nMessages from the component orchestrating the HTTP and Websocket servers that power the preview functionality\n\n&#96;esbonio.PreviewServer&#96;\n\nRecords the HTTP traffic from the server that serves the HTML files built by Sphinx\n\n&#96;esbonio.SphinxManager&#96;\n\nMessages from the component that manages the server’s underlying Sphinx processes\n\n&#96;esbonio.WebviewServer&#96;\n\nMessages about the websocket connection between the HTML viewer and the server\n\n&#96;py.warnings&#96;\n\nLog messages coming from Python’s warnings framework\n\n&#96;sphinx&#96;\n\nLog messages coming from an underlying sphinx process\n\n### Sphinx¶\n\nThe following options control the creation and management of background Sphinx process used by the server\n\npyproject.toml\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n[tool.esbonio.sphinx]\nbuildCommand = [\"sphinx-build\", \"-M\", \"dirhtml\", \"docs\", \"docs/_build\"]\nconfigOverrides = { html_theme = \"alabaster\", language = \"cy\" }\npythonCommand = [\"uv\", \"run\", \"python\"]\n\n# Alternatively for more control over how the background Sphinx process is launched.\n#\n# [tool.esbonio.sphinx.pythonCommand]\n# command = [\"hatch\", \"-e\", \"docs\", \"run\", \"python\"]\n# cwd = \"${scopeFsPath}/docs\"\n# env = {MYENVVAR = \"value\"}\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nsettings.json (VSCode only)\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n{\n  \"esbonio.sphinx.buildCommand\": [\n     \"sphinx-build\", \"-M\", \"dirhtml\", \"docs\", \"docs/_build\"\n  ],\n  \"esbonio.sphinx.configOverrides\": {\n      \"html_theme\": \"alabaster\",\n      \"language\": \"cy\",\n  },\n  \"esbonio.sphinx.pythonCommand\": {\n     \"command\": [\"uv\", \"run\", \"python\"],\n     \"cwd\": \"${scopeFsPath}\",\n     \"env\": {\n         \"MYENVVAR\": \"value\"\n     }\n  }\n\n  \"esbonio.sphinx.buildTriggers\": { \"onSave\": true, \"onChange\": 2.0 }\n}\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nproject string[] esbonio.sphinx.buildArguments¶\n\nThe&#96;sphinx-build&#96; command line arguments&#96;esbonio&#96; should use when building your documentation. For more information, see [How To Configure the Sphinx Build](https://docs.esbon.io/en/latest/usage/howto/configure-the-sphinx-build-cmd.html#lsp-configure-sphinx-build-cmd)\n\nNote\n\nThis must be the genuine command line for&#96;sphinx-build&#96;. Wrapper scripts around&#96;sphinx-build&#96; are not supported.\n\nproject string[] esbonio.sphinx.buildCommand¶\n\nAlias for&#96;esbonio.sphinx.buildArguments&#96;. If both&#96;buildArguments&#96; and&#96;buildCommand&#96; are provided, the value for&#96;buildArguments&#96; will take priority.\n\nproject object esbonio.sphinx.configOverrides¶\n\nThis option can be used to override values set in the project’s&#96;conf.py&#96; file. This can be used to replace both the [sphinx-build -D](https://www.sphinx-doc.org/en/master/man/sphinx-build.html#cmdoption-sphinx-build-D) and [sphinx-build -A](https://www.sphinx-doc.org/en/master/man/sphinx-build.html#cmdoption-sphinx-build-A) cli options.\n\nSee [How To Configure the Sphinx Build](https://docs.esbon.io/en/latest/usage/howto/configure-the-sphinx-build-cmd.html#lsp-configure-sphinx-build-cmd) for details\n\nproject string[] esbonio.sphinx.pythonCommand¶\n\nInstructs&#96;esbonio&#96; how to launch the Python interpreter it uses for the background Sphinx process. Use this option to ensure that the correct Python environment for your documentation is selected.\n\nThis can be as simple as the full path to the Python executable in your virtual environment:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n[\"/home/user/Projects/example/venv/bin/python\"]\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nOr a complex command with a number of options and arguments:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n[\"hatch\", \"-e\", \"docs\", \"run\", \"python\"]\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nIf the command is not&#96;python&#96;, then it must accept additional parameters (e.g.&#96;-m sphinx&#96;) and pass these to the Python interpreter. The command must not replace or clear the environment variable&#96;PYTHONPATH&#96;, it may, however, extend it with additional entries.\n\nFor more examples see [How To Configure the Sphinx Build Environment](https://docs.esbon.io/en/latest/usage/howto/configure-the-sphinx-build-env.html#lsp-configure-sphinx-build-env)\n\nproject string esbonio.sphinx.pythonCommand.cwd¶\n\nThe working directory from which to launch the Sphinx process. If not set\n\n&#96;esbonio&#96; will use the directory containing the closest&#96;pyproject.toml&#96; file.\n\nIf no&#96;pyproject.toml&#96; file can be found,&#96;esbonio&#96; will use workspace folder containing the project.\n\nproject object esbonio.sphinx.pythonCommand.env¶\n\nAdditional environment variables to set for the background Sphinx process\n\nglobal object esbonio.sphinx.buildTriggers¶\n\nThis option controls when the language server rebuilds your documentation. The server’s default configuration is equivalent to setting\n\nVSCode\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n{\n  \"esbonio.sphinx.buildTriggers\": {\n    \"onSave\": true,\n    \"onChange\": 2.0,\n  }\n}\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nwhere&#96;esbonio&#96; will rebuild each time you save a file, or each time you modify a file after a delay of 2 seconds.\n\nThe following configuration will disable all builds\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n{\n  \"esbonio.sphinx.buildTriggers\": {\n    \"onSave\": false,\n    \"onChange\": false,\n  }\n}\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n### Preview¶\n\nThe following options control the behavior of the HTML preview\n\nsettings.json (VSCode only)\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n{\n    \"esbonio.preview.bind\": \"localhost\",\n    \"esbonio.preview.httpPort\": 1234,\n    \"esbonio.preview.wsPort\": 0,\n    \"esbonio.preview.synchronizeScroll\": \"bothWays\",\n}\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nglobal string esbonio.preview.bind¶\n\nThe network interface to bind the preview server to.\n\nglobal integer esbonio.preview.httpPort¶\n\nThe port number to bind the HTTP server to. {
  "url": "https://docs.esbon.io/en/latest/usage/howto/configure-the-sphinx-build-cmd.html",
  "markdown": "How To Configure the Sphinx Build - Esbonio\n\n# How To Configure the Sphinx Build¶\n\nUnless told otherwise,&#96;esbonio&#96; will try to guess a sensible&#96;sphinx-build&#96; command that it will use when building your documentation. This command will be adjusted to match your project’s structure, but it will be something like the following:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\nsphinx-build -M dirhtml docs ${defaultBuildDir}\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nwhere the&#96;${defaultBuildDir}&#96; variable expands into a subfolder of your user’s cache directory, as determined by [platformdirs](https://platformdirs.readthedocs.io/en/latest/).\n\nOf course, the&#96;sphinx-build&#96; command you use with your project may be different from the default, in which case you can set the [esbonio.sphinx.buildArguments](https://docs.esbon.io/en/latest/usage/reference/configuration.html#esbonio.sphinx.buildArguments) option in your project’s&#96;pyproject.toml&#96; to override this.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n[tool.esbonio.sphinx]\nbuildArguments = [\"sphinx-build\", \"-M\", \"html\", \"docs\", \"${defaultBuildDir}\", \"--nitpicky\", \"--verbose\"]\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n[esbonio.sphinx.buildArguments](https://docs.esbon.io/en/latest/usage/reference/configuration.html#esbonio.sphinx.buildArguments) must be the genuine command line for&#96;sphinx-build&#96;. Wrapper scripts around&#96;sphinx-build&#96;, e.g. a Makefile, are not supported.\n\nWhy use&#96;${defaultBuildDir}&#96;?\n\nThere are two main reasons why&#96;esbonio&#96; will store its build output in your user’s cache directory by default.\n\nThere’s no risk of&#96;esbonio&#96; accidentally clobbering files and folders in your project’s structure.\n\nEsbonio’s build output is not the same as the output produced by a standalone&#96;sphinx-build&#96;. This is becasue&#96;esbonio&#96;\n\nInjects [custom HTML and JavaScript](https://github.com/swyddfa/esbonio/blob/develop/lib/esbonio/esbonio/sphinx_agent/static/webview.js) in order to support its synchronised scrolling feature\n\nProduces additional output files like the&#96;esbonio.db&#96; SQLite file that supports most of its LSP features.\n\n## Supported Options¶\n\n&#96;esbonio&#96; supports almost every option supported by the [sphinx-build](https://www.sphinx-doc.org/en/master/man/sphinx-build.html) command. The only exceptions are the options that do not make sense in the context of a language server, including\n\n&#96;--color&#96;/&#96;--no-color&#96;,&#96;esbonio&#96; will always disable colors in the Sphinx processes it spawns\n\n&#96;-P&#96;/&#96;--pdb&#96;, as there is no way for the user to interact with esbonio’s background processes, there is no point launching a debugger when an exception is encountered.\n\n## Relative Filepaths¶\n\nFilepaths like&#96;.&#96; or&#96;docs&#96; in a&#96;sphinx-build&#96; command obviously need to be resolved relative to some “current directory”. When using a&#96;pyproject.toml&#96; file, the current directory is set to the parent folder for example, say your project had the following structure:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n$ tree myproject\nmyproject\n ├── docs\n │   └── conf.py\n └── pyproject.toml\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nThen your [esbonio.sphinx.buildArguments](https://docs.esbon.io/en/latest/usage/reference/configuration.html#esbonio.sphinx.buildArguments) might look something like\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n[tool.esbonio.sphinx]\nbuildArguments = [\"sphinx-build\", \"-M\", \"html\", \"docs\", \"${defaultBuildDir}\"]\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nIf you set the build command using the settings in your editor, the “current directory” will be set to root of your workspace\n\n## Config Overrides¶\n\nSphinx allows you to override options in your project’s&#96;conf.py&#96; on the command line using the [-D](https://www.sphinx-doc.org/en/master/man/sphinx-build.html#cmdoption-sphinx-build-D) or&#96;--define&#96; flags. While you can provide these direct in your build command,&#96;esbonio&#96; offers a dedicated [esbonio.sphinx.configOverrides](https://docs.esbon.io/en/latest/usage/reference/configuration.html#esbonio.sphinx.configOverrides) option where you can set these.\n\nFor example, to override the theme used by the project\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n[tool.esbonio.sphinx]\nbuildArguments = [\"sphinx-build\", \"-M\", \"dirhtml\", \".\", \"${defaultBuildDir}\"]\nconfigOverrides = { html_theme = \"alabaster\" }\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nThough of course, this setting probably make most sense to be set via your editor.\n\n## HTML Overrides¶\n\nSimiarly, using the [-A](https://www.sphinx-doc.org/en/master/man/sphinx-build.html#cmdoption-sphinx-build-A) or&#96;--html-define&#96; flags you can override values that are passed to your HTML templates. The [esbonio.sphinx.configOverrides](https://docs.esbon.io/en/latest/usage/reference/configuration.html#esbonio.sphinx.configOverrides) option can also be used instead of these flags - provided that the values are nested inside a&#96;html_context&#96;“configuration option”.\n\nFor example, there is a HTML template variable called&#96;docstitle&#96; that sets the title of the current page. To override it to be&#96;My Custom Title&#96; you could use the following\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n[tool.esbonio.sphinx]\nbuildArguments = [\"sphinx-build\", \"-M\", \"dirhtml\", \".\", \"${defaultBuildDir}\"]\nconfigOverrides = { html_context.docstitle = \"My Custom Title\" }\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nThough of course, this setting probably make most sense to be set via your editor."
}
```

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
**Analyzing esbonio build behavior**

I found that esbonio stores build output in the user's cache directory using `${defaultBuildDir}` and injects custom HTML/JS for synchronized scrolling. I realized that specifying build directory explicitly overrides the default cache directory behavior. Esbonio modifies the build process to inject custom HTML and JS, so running `make html` manually won't include these additions. </details></think>

[Changelog #](https://docs.esbon.io/en/esbonio-language-server-v0.16.4/changelog.html)<br/>4. [Configuration - Esbonio](https://docs.esbon.io/en/release/lsp/reference/configuration.html)<br/>5. [How To Integrate Esbonio with Neovim ¶](https://docs.esbon.io/en/latest/integrating/howto/nvim.html)<br/><br/># Esbonio Preview Settings: buildDir in settings.json vs build<br/><br/>Based on the Esbonio documentation, `buildDir` is a **project-scoped Sphinx configuration option** that specifies where Sphinx outputs compiled documentation.[4]<br/><br/>## Configuration Sources<br/><br/>Esbonio reads configuration from multiple sources in priority order:[4]<br/>1. `workspace/configuration` (highest priority)<br/>2. `pyproject.toml` files<br/>3. `initializationOptions` (lowest priority)<br/><br/>## buildDir in Different Contexts<br/><br/>**In VS Code settings.json (via initializationOptions):**<br/>The `buildDir` can be set in VS Code's settings under `initializationOptions` > `sphinx` > `buildDir`. This is shown in a real example where a user set it to `/Users/will/Library/CloudStorage/Dropbox/research/macro/pyscope/docs/source/_build`.[2]<br/><br/>**In build directory:**<br/>The `buildDir` points to the actual output directory where Sphinx generates HTML and other compiled files. This is essential for the preview functionality to work, as the language server needs to locate the compiled output files to display previews.[1][2]<br/><br/>## Key Difference<br/><br/>Settings in `settings.json` (workspace/configuration requests) have **higher priority** than `initializationOptions`, so if you define `buildDir` in both places, the `settings.json` value will override the initialization option.[4] |

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
**Reviewing esbonio docs**

I'm checking the esbonio documentation to understand how it modifies sphinx-build behavior for preview rendering. 

**Analyzing esbonio integration**

Esbonio runs sphinx-build internally but modifies the process with custom HTML/JS injection and additional output files like esbonio.db. Esbonio must run its own build to generate correct preview files, since pre-built files lack required JS injection. 

**Fixing preview server**

I'm investigating why the preview server serves from the wrong directory and handles CORS/iframes incorrectly. </details></think>

---

Yapılandırmayı güncelledim. Yapılan değişiklikler:

## Yapılan Değişiklikler

### 1. `.vscode/settings.json`
- `esbonio.sphinx.buildCommand` eklendi; **mutlak yollar** kullanılıyor.
- `source` ve `build` artık proje köküne göre değil, tam yol ile belirtiliyor.

### 2. `pyproject.toml`
- `buildCommand` aynı şekilde mutlak yollarla güncellendi.

Böylece Esbonio:
- `conf.py`’nin `source/` içinde olduğunu,
- HTML çıktısının `build/html/` altında olduğunu,
- Kaynak → HTML eşlemesini doğru kurar.

---

## Şimdi Denemeniz Gerekenler

1. **Esbonio’yu yeniden başlatın**  
   Cmd+Shift+P → **"Esbonio: Restart Language Server"** veya **"Esbonio: Restart Sphinx Process"**

2. **Preview’ı tekrar çalıştırın**  
   Bir `.rst` dosyası açıkken Cmd+Shift+P → **"Esbonio: Preview Documentation in Split Window"**

3. **Debug log’a bakın**  
   View → Output → **Esbonio** ile hata mesajlarını kontrol edin.

---

[Esbonio - Visual Studio Marketplace](https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=swyddfa.esbonio)<br/><br/># Esbonio Server Process Runtime Error in VSCode<br/><br/>Esbonio is a language server for Sphinx projects that integrates with VSCode.[2] Users have reported several "Unable to start language server" runtime errors related to the server process.<br/><br/>## Common Issues<br/><br/>**Server Startup Failures**: Users encounter errors like "esbonio: Failed" or "Unable to start language server" in VSCode, with minimal debugging information in the output window.[3][4] This has been reported particularly on Windows systems, while Linux installations work fine.[4]<br/><br/>**Entry Point Configuration**: Early versions had a misconfigured entry point issue where running `esbonio` directly would fail with a `TypeError: main() missing 1 required positional argument`.[3] This was identified and fixed in PR #350.[3]<br/><br/>**Cursor/Preview Issues on Codespaces**: There's a reported issue where Esbonio Preview doesn't work properly on GitHub Codespaces, even when the build process completes and the port is forwarded.[1]<br/><br/>## Troubleshooting Steps<br/><br/>- Set `esbonio.server.logLevel = "debug"` in VSCode settings to get more detailed error messages[3]<br/>- Verify the Python interpreter is correctly configured through the Python Extension[2]<br/>- Ensure esbonio is properly installed in your project's virtual environment<br/>- Check that you're using `python3 -m esbonio` or the correct entry point configuration[3]<br/><br/>The extension has 58,999 installs and generally positive ratings on the VSCode Marketplace.[5] |

[Esbonio](https://docs.esbon.io/en/latest)<br/><br/># Esbonio 1.0 Configuration with pyproject.toml and buildCommand<br/><br/>## Configuration Sources<br/><br/>Esbonio 1.0 supports reading configuration from `pyproject.toml` files with **project scope**.[1] The configuration system follows a priority hierarchy where `pyproject.toml` settings are overridden by workspace/configuration requests but take precedence over initialization options.[1]<br/><br/>## buildCommand Configuration<br/><br/>The `esbonio.sphinx.buildCommand` option is used to configure the Sphinx build command. In v1.0, passing the `-C` flag to this option now works as expected, enabling the use of esbonio with files outside of Sphinx projects.[3]<br/><br/>## pythonCommand Enhancements in v1.0<br/><br/>In version 1.0, `esbonio.sphinx.pythonCommand` was enhanced from a simple list of strings to an object with multiple fields:[3]<br/>- `command`: List of strings (the original format)<br/>- `env`: Dictionary for setting/overriding environment variables<br/>- `cwd`: String to change the directory where the sphinx process launches<br/><br/>The server maintains backward compatibility by still accepting a list of strings, which are automatically converted to the new object format.[3]<br/><br/>If no `pythonCommand` is configured, esbonio 1.0 will attempt to use the server's runtime environment as a fallback.[3] |

Özet ve adımlar:

## Yapılan Değişiklikler

### 1. **`esbonio.server.pythonPath` eklendi**
Esbonio’ya hangi Python’u kullanacağı net şekilde söyleniyor. Bu, “server process” hatasını önleyebilir.

### 2. **Esbonio 0.16.5 → 1.1.0 yükseltildi**
- 0.16.x artık geliştirilmiyor; 1.x öneriliyor
- 1.1.0 Python 3.13 ile uyumlu
- LSP ve süreç yönetimi iyileştirilmiş

---

## Şimdi Yapmanız Gerekenler

1. **Cursor’u yeniden başlatın** (Cmd+Q ile kapatıp tekrar açın veya **Developer: Reload Window**)
2. Bir `.rst` dosyası açın ve Esbonio’nun başlamasını bekleyin
3. **Esbonio: Preview Documentation** komutunu deneyin

---

## Hâlâ Hata Alırsanız

**Esbonio Output** (View → Output → Esbonio) panelindeki çıktıyı paylaşın.

Olası kontroller:

- Esbonio **VS Code eklentisi** sürümü (1.x sunucusu için 0.9x veya 1.x olmalı)
- **Extensions** panelinden Esbonio eklentisini güncelleme

Sunucu hatası büyük olasılıkla eski Esbonio ve Python 3.13 uyumsuzluğundan kaynaklanıyordu; 1.1.0 ile çözülmüş olmalı.

Özet ve sonraki adımlar:

## Yapılan Değişiklikler

1. **`esbonio.server.startupModule": "esbonio"`** – Modül `esbonio.server` yerine `esbonio` olarak ayarlandı (farklı başlatma yolu).
2. **`pyproject.toml`** – Olası sorun çıkaran `readme` satırı kaldırıldı.

---

## Hata Tespiti

**RuntimeError: Server process exited with return code: 2** hatası, Esbonio süreci başlıyor ama hemen kapanıyor demek.

Önce şunu yapın:

1. **Cursor’u açmadan önce** View → Output’u açın ve listeden **Esbonio**’yu seçin.
2. Cursor’da Belgeler klasörünü açın.
3. Hata oluştuğunda Output panelinde **Esbonio** sekmesinde görünen tüm çıktıyı (özellikle traceback/hata mesajlarını) paylaşın.

---

## Geçici Çözüm: Esbonio’yu Devre Dışı Bırakma

Cursor açılışında hata almamak için Esbonio’yu kapatabilirsiniz:

```json
"esbonio.server.enabled": false
```

Bu ayarı `.vscode/settings.json` içine ekleyin. Gerekirse tekrar `true` yapıp **Esbonio: Restart Language Server** ile başlatabilirsiniz.

---

## Olası Nedenler ve Denenebilecekler

- **Cursor vs VS Code**: Cursor farklı davranabiliyor; aynı projeyi VS Code ile açıp Esbonio’nun çalışıp çalışmadığını kontrol edin.
- **Başlatma sırası**: Önce başka bir klasör açıp, sonra Belgeler’i ekleyerek açmayı deneyin; Esbonio bazen daha geç başladığında sorun kaybolabiliyor.
- **Eklenti sürümü**: Extensions panelinden Esbonio’yu kaldırıp yeniden yüklemeyi deneyin.

Output panelindeki Esbonio çıktısını paylaşırsanız, hatanın nedeni daha net belirlenebilir.

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Now I see the problem! The Esbonio VS Code extension starts the server with `python -S` flag, which disables site-packages. It relies on PYTHONPATH being set to its bundled libs directory to find esbonio. 

But the bundled libs don't include Sphinx and other project dependencies (sphinx_design, sphinx_tabs, etc.). So the server itself starts OK from the bundled libs, but when it tries to spawn the Sphinx subprocess, it uses `pythonCommand` from the config.

The key issue is: the extension runs the server with `-S` flag and sets `PYTHONPATH` to the bundled libs. The server finds esbonio there. But when it spawns the Sphinx subprocess, it uses the `pythonCommand` from the config.


The extension constructs the startup command by combining the Python executable with the `-S` flag and the esbonio server module, which means the bundled libs are available to the server process itself, but the `-S` flag still blocks access to the conda environment's site-packages where Sphinx and its extensions are actually installed.

Now I'm trying to figure out what could trigger an exit code 2 — that's typically a command-line argument error or some kind of initialization failure in the startup module.

Looking at the esbonio CLI, it uses argparse which exits with code 2 when it encounters invalid arguments. The extension is building a command that includes the startup module and then passes various include/exclude module arguments, so I need to check what arguments are actually being sent to see if something's malformed or unexpected. The `esbonio.server.pythonPath` setting determines which Python executable gets used, and when combined with the `-S` flag and module name, it constructs the full command array. I'm wondering if `pythonCommand` might contain unexpected elements from the `getCmd` method, or if the issue is actually in how the exact command is being assembled with the PYTHONPATH environment variable and the Python path from the miniforge environment.

Looking at the actual command being executed, the language server should start and listen for LSP messages over stdin, but the exit code 2 suggests it's failing immediately. I suspect the bundled libraries might be missing a critical dependency—specifically `websockets`, which esbonio needs but doesn't appear to be included in the bundled libs alongside the other packages like aiosqlite, docutils, platformdirs, and pygls. I'm realizing the core issue is likely a missing dependency in the bundled libraries—if `websockets` isn't included, the import will fail even with the correct PYTHONPATH setup. Beyond that, I've been overcomplicating the configuration with conflicting settings between pyproject.toml and settings.json, plus that custom `startupModule` change that might not be compatible with this extension version. I should strip away the unnecessary customizations and let the extension run with its defaults.

The real problem is understanding the execution flow: the extension launches the language server with `-S` (which blocks site-packages), so it only has access to bundled libraries. Then esbonio reads pyproject.toml to determine how to spawn the separate Sphinx subprocess, which is a different process entirely with its own Python environment requirements.

Let me step back and simplify—I'll strip the settings down to just what's necessary, remove any overrides, and test whether the exact command the extension would run actually works.</details></think>

Yapılandırma temiz. Sorunun özeti:

## Bulunan Sorun

**Global Cursor ayarlarında** (`~/Library/Application Support/Cursor/User/settings.json`) eski ve yanlış yollar vardı:

| Ayar | Eski (hatalı) | Düzeltme |
|------|---------------|----------|
| `esbonio.sphinx.pythonPath` | `/Users/ismailguzel/miniconda3/envs/truba-belgeler/bin/python` | `[]` (boş) |
| `esbonio.sphinx.buildCommand` | `["sphinx-build"]` (eksik) | `[]` (workspace'e bırakıldı) |

`ismailguzel` ve `miniconda3` yolları bu makinede mevcut değil; Esbonio bu yolu PYTHONPATH'e eklemeye çalışırken hata alıyordu.

## Şu Anki Yapılandırma

- **Global ayarlar**: Sorunlu esbonio yolları temizlendi
- **Workspace** (`.vscode/settings.json`): Sadece Python yolu + debug log
- **`pyproject.toml`**: Esbonio'ya `pythonCommand` (conda env Python) ve `buildCommand` (sphinx-build) verildi

## Sonraki Adım

**Cursor'u tamamen kapatıp yeniden açın** (Cmd+Q). Esbonio yeni ayarlarla başlamalı; hata mesajı kaybolmalı.

## Sorunun Kök Nedeni

**Global Cursor ayarlarında** eski bir makineden kalan yanlış yollar vardı:

```
esbonio.sphinx.pythonPath → /Users/ismailguzel/miniconda3/envs/truba-belgeler/bin/python
```

Bu yol mevcut makinede (`iguzel` / `miniforge3`) bulunmadığı için Esbonio sunucu süreci başlatılamıyordu ve `RuntimeError: Server process exited with return code: 2` hatası oluşuyordu.

## Yapılan Düzeltmeler

1. **Global ayarlar** (`~/Library/Application Support/Cursor/User/settings.json`) — hatalı `esbonio.sphinx.pythonPath` ve eksik `esbonio.sphinx.buildCommand`
